book_claims.claim: Used in several Longevity Diet soups and stews; provides iron (2 mg per medium, 11% DV), magnesium (43 mg per 3.5 oz baked with skin, 11% DV), vitamin C (17 mg per medium baked, 28% DV), and calcium (62 mg per cup baked with salt, 6% DV). Sweet potato is a top vitamin A source (1403 mcg RAE per whole baked in skin, 561% DV).
book_claims.mechanism: Starchy vegetable; sweet potato exceptionally rich in beta-carotene (vitamin A provitamin)

supplementary_research.finding: Cooling cooked potatoes significantly increases resistant starch (RS2/RS3), lowering glycemic response by up to 40%; meal prep strategy relevant to longevity diet
Sweet potato anthocyanins and beta-carotene demonstrate anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects; associated with reduced oxidative stress markers in interventional studies

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble; absorption significantly enhanced by eating with olive oil
Highest potassium of common starchy foods; supports cardiovascular function
Acts as prebiotic; lowers glycemic response compared to freshly cooked

synergies.description: Fat dramatically improves beta-carotene absorption from sweet potato; also lowers glycemic index of regular potato
Potatoes add carbohydrates and potassium while legumes provide protein; traditional blue-zone soup combination
Book uses potatoes as starchy base in soups and stews alongside vegetables for balanced longevity meals
